title: Polish reify-mcp crate: validation hardening, derives, error handling
analysis:
The reify-mcp crate is a complete MCP server with 16 tools, a JSON-RPC 2.0 dispatcher, tool registry, and dual-mode transport (in-process + async streams). It has 76 existing tests across 8 test modules. This polish task addresses 5 issues found during code review:

S1 (PartialEq): All types in types.rs lack PartialEq. All inner types (String, u32, bool, Option<String>, Vec<String>, Option<f64>) support PartialEq. ToolError uses thiserror but all variants contain only Strings, so PartialEq is derivable.

S2 (JSON-RPC validation): The dispatcher currently trusts the parsed request without validating jsonrpc version or ID type. Per JSON-RPC 2.0 spec, jsonrpc MUST be '2.0' and id SHOULD be string or number. We need INVALID_REQUEST (-32600), INVALID_PARAMS (-32602), and INTERNAL_ERROR (-32603) constants. The arguments key in tools/call should be validated as an object.

S3 (Error handling): handle_tools_call() uses unwrap_or('') for name extraction (line 121) and unwrap_or_default() for result serialization (line 127). The first silently treats missing/non-string name as empty string leading to confusing 'unknown tool: ' error. The second silently converts serialization failures to empty string. Both should produce proper error responses.

S4 (Duplicate registration): register() silently appends duplicates to the Vec. Since tool registration is a startup concern, panicking on duplicates is appropriate — it's a programming error. This avoids changing the return type signature and churn across 16 tool registration calls.

S5 (Transport logging): run_on_streams() silently swallows I/O errors via .is_err() checks and break. Since the workspace has no logging framework, we'll use eprintln! to stderr for debug visibility and change the return type to Result<(), io::Error> so callers can react to transport failures.

steps.description: S1: Write test in registry_tests.rs that asserts PartialEq on all MCP types — SourceContent, OpenFileInfo, DiagnosticInfo, ParameterInfo, ConstraintInfo, EvalStatusInfo, SelectionInfo, SourceLocationInfo, UpdateResult, SetParamResult, ToolInfo, ToolError. Uses == comparisons that won't compile without PartialEq derives. Create two instances of each type and assert_eq! them.

S1: Add PartialEq to all derive macros in types.rs. For ToolError: add PartialEq to the derive list (thiserror + PartialEq is fine since all variants contain only String). For all struct types (ToolInfo, SourceContent, OpenFileInfo, DiagnosticInfo, ParameterInfo, ConstraintInfo, EvalStatusInfo, SelectionInfo, SourceLocationInfo, UpdateResult, SetParamResult): add PartialEq to their derive macros.

S2: Write tests in jsonrpc_tests.rs for JSON-RPC request validation: (a) dispatch_invalid_jsonrpc_version_returns_error — request with jsonrpc:'1.0' returns error code -32600. (b) dispatch_null_id_returns_invalid_request — request with id:null returns -32600. (c) dispatch_array_id_returns_invalid_request — request with id:[1,2] returns -32600. All three use existing setup_registry() and MockToolContext.

S2: Add INVALID_REQUEST (-32600), INVALID_PARAMS (-32602), INTERNAL_ERROR (-32603) pub(crate) constants to jsonrpc.rs. In dispatch(), after successful JSON parse: validate jsonrpc == '2.0' (return INVALID_REQUEST if not), validate id is string or number (return INVALID_REQUEST if null/array/object/bool). Use existing error_response() helper for both.

S2+S3: Write tests in jsonrpc_tests.rs for tools/call validation: (a) dispatch_tools_call_missing_name_returns_invalid_params — tools/call with params:{arguments:{}} (no name field) returns isError content with message mentioning 'name'. (b) dispatch_tools_call_numeric_name_returns_invalid_params — tools/call with params:{name:123, arguments:{}} returns isError content. (c) dispatch_tools_call_array_arguments_returns_invalid_params — tools/call with params:{name:'reify_get_source', arguments:[1,2]} returns isError content mentioning 'arguments'.

S2+S3: In handle_tools_call(): Replace unwrap_or('') with explicit match — if params['name'] is not a string, return an MCP isError content response with 'missing or invalid tool name'. Validate arguments: if present and not null, must be an object (reject arrays/strings/numbers with 'arguments must be an object'). If null or missing, default to empty object {}. Replace unwrap_or_default() on serde_json::to_string with .map_err to return an INTERNAL_ERROR JsonRpcResponse on serialization failure.

S4: Write test in registry_tests.rs: registry_duplicate_name_panics — register a tool 'alpha', then register another tool with the same name 'alpha'. Use #[should_panic(expected = "duplicate")] to verify the panic message.

S4: In ToolRegistry::register(), add at the top: if self.tools.iter().any(|e| e.info.name == name), panic with format!("duplicate tool registration: '{}'", name). This catches programming errors at startup.

S5: Write tests in transport_tests.rs: (a) stream_mode_write_error_returns_err — create an McpServer, use run_on_streams with a valid reader containing one JSON-RPC request but a writer that always returns io::Error. Assert the returned Result is Err. (b) stream_mode_graceful_eof_returns_ok — run_on_streams with an empty reader (immediate EOF), assert Result is Ok (graceful shutdown on clean EOF).

S5: Change run_on_streams() return type from () to Result<(), std::io::Error>. On write_all error: eprintln! the error, return Err(e). On flush error: eprintln! the error, return Err(e). On clean loop exit (EOF): return Ok(()). On read error (lines.next_line() returns Err): eprintln! and return Err(e) — change while let pattern to handle Err case explicitly. Update run_stdio() to call .await and log any error with eprintln! (fire-and-forget for CLI mode). Update existing stream_mode_tools_list test to .unwrap() the new Result.

design_decisions.decision: S4: Panic on duplicate tool registration instead of returning Result
design_decisions.rationale: Duplicate registration is a programming error (all 16 tools are registered at startup in register_all_tools). Panicking avoids changing the register() return type which would require updating all 16 registration call sites and the register_all_tools signature. This follows the Rust convention of using panics for invariant violations that indicate bugs.

design_decisions.decision: S5: Use eprintln! for transport error logging instead of adding a logging framework
design_decisions.rationale: The workspace has no logging framework (no tracing, log, or env_logger in any Cargo.toml). Adding one for a single crate's debug output would be disproportionate. eprintln! to stderr is sufficient for MCP transport debugging and is the simplest correct solution.

design_decisions.decision: S5: Change run_on_streams return type to Result<(), io::Error>
design_decisions.rationale: Returning Result lets callers distinguish between graceful shutdown (client disconnected, EOF) and I/O failures. run_stdio() will log errors to stderr. This surfaces errors that were previously silently swallowed.

design_decisions.decision: S2: Treat null ID as invalid request
design_decisions.rationale: Per JSON-RPC 2.0 spec, null id means the server MUST NOT reply (notification). However MCP initialize/tools/list/tools/call all require responses. Rejecting null IDs is correct MCP behavior. If notification support is needed later, it can be added with method-specific handling.

design_decisions.decision: S3: Missing/non-string name returns MCP content error, not JSON-RPC error
design_decisions.rationale: Following the existing pattern where tool-level errors (unknown tool, missing params) are returned as MCP isError content, not JSON-RPC-level errors. This is consistent with how the crate differentiates transport-level vs tool-level errors. Unknown tool already follows this pattern.

design_decisions.decision: S2: Non-object arguments is rejected; null/missing defaults to empty object
design_decisions.rationale: When arguments is missing (null from JSON indexing), treating it as empty object {} is reasonable since many tools accept no arguments. But if arguments is present as a non-object type (array, string, number), that's an explicit protocol error worth reporting.

reuse.how: Use existing MockToolContext::default() for setting up test dispatchers and registries, following the pattern in existing test files
Reuse the existing setup_registry() helper that creates a minimal 2-tool registry for dispatcher tests
Use the existing error_response() method to generate properly formatted JSON-RPC error responses for validation failures
Use existing InvalidParams variant for name/arguments validation, following the pattern already used in tool handlers
Follow the existing stream_mode_tools_list test's duplex channel pattern for testing stream behavior, plus a custom failing writer struct implementing AsyncWrite
